cards.text: Rise, take the child and his mother, and flee to Egypt...
cards.connection: Shows God's providential use of Egypt as a safe refuge for the infant Jesus, echoing Old Testament themes.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing Egypt's early connection to the Gospel narrative.

cards.text: There the centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing for Italy and put us on board.
cards.connection: Illustrates how God used the Roman grain trade infrastructure to facilitate the spread of the Gospel.
cards.cueContext: Used to explain the providential aspect of the Alexandrian grain trade.

cards.text: Now a Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, came to Ephesus. He was an eloquent man, competent in the Scriptures.
cards.connection: Demonstrates the intellectual and rhetorical training available to Alexandrian Jews that benefited the early church.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing Alexandria's intellectual climate.

cards.text: If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me.
cards.connection: Embodies the spirit of the Egyptian martyrs who refused to compromise their allegiance to Christ.
cards.cueContext: Used when reflecting on the era of the Great Persecutions.

cards.text: ...These have indeed an appearance of wisdom in promoting self-made religion and asceticism and severity to the body, but they are of no value in stopping the indulgence of the flesh.
cards.connection: Provides a biblical warning against the dangers of extreme monastic asceticism becoming an end in itself.
cards.cueContext: Shown during the theological reflection on the desert monastic movement.

cards.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
cards.connection: The foundational biblical truth that Athanasius defended against the Arian heresy at Nicaea.
cards.cueContext: Used to clarify the core theological issue of the Arian controversy.
